one_line_takeaway: Adds an error-feedback DP-SGD variant, generator noise injection, and an L2 reconstruction loss on top of DP-GAN-DPAC/GS-WGAN, giving the largest quality/utility gains on the harder CelebA benchmark (FID 139.99->114.03, IS 1.90->2.26) at the same fixed privacy budget (epsilon=10, delta=1e-5), with marginal gains on the already near-saturated MNIST benchmark.
key_finding: On Table 1 (IS/FID at epsilon=10, delta=1e-5): CelebA showed the largest gains -- IS improved from DP-GAN-DPAC's 1.90 to 2.26 and FID improved from 139.99 to 114.03 (vs. GS-WGAN 1.85/187.36 and DPSinkhorn -/168.40). Fashion-MNIST: IS 6.60->6.72, FID 90.77->83.48 versus DP-GAN-DPAC. MNIST: smaller gains, IS 9.71->9.74, FID 54.06->49.41. Table 2 (gen2real accuracy, MLP/CNN): CelebA improved most notably for CNN (DP-GAN-DPAC 0.83 -> Ours 0.88), while MLP accuracy tied DP-GAN-DPAC on CelebA (0.80) and Fashion-MNIST-CNN was slightly lower than DP-GAN-DPAC (0.70 vs 0.71). Ablations (Tables 3-4, baseline = DP-GAN-DPAC settings with sigma_noise=0/gamma_recon=0): noise injection at sigma_noise=0.1 improved FID on MNIST (54.6->49.41) and CelebA (139.99->97.70) but sigma_noise=1.0 degraded Fashion-MNIST and CelebA FID (102.62 and 218.38, both worse than baseline), showing a quality/diversity trade-off with noise magnitude. Reconstruction loss (gamma_recon=1.0) improved IS and FID on all three datasets versus baseline (e.g., CelebA FID 139.99->99.10).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a differentially private (DP) image-generation framework built on top of GS-WGAN and the prior state-of-the-art DP-GAN-DPAC. It contributes three own elements: (1) 'Error Feedback SGD' (EFSGD), a DP-SGD variant that maintains separate accumulated clipping-error variables (e^t_d, e^t_c, e^t_e) for the discriminator, classifier, and encoder in a multi-component generator training pipeline, feeding the clipped error back into the next update to offset gradient-clipping bias; (2) a Gaussian noise-injection mechanism added element-wise to the generator's feature maps after each upsampling layer (analogous to StyleGAN noise injection) to increase sample diversity/quality under the same privacy budget; and (3) an L2-norm reconstruction loss (via an added VAE-style encoder, Eq. 14) folded into the generator's total loss (Eq. 15) to preserve structural detail lost to DP noise. Only the resulting private generator is released; discriminators, classifier, and encoder are kept private, following the GS-WGAN release pattern.
threat_model: Standard DP generative-model threat model inherited from DP-SGD/GS-WGAN/DP-GAN-DPAC: an adversary with white- or black-box access to a generative model trained on sensitive data (e.g., medical records, facial images) could mount model inversion attacks (MIA, citing Fredrikson et al. and Khosravy et al.) or membership inference attacks (citing Shokri et al., Webster et al.) to recover or confirm individual training samples. The paper's mechanism provides (epsilon, delta)-differential privacy guarantees (Eq. 1-2) via a Gaussian sanitization mechanism on gradients, accounted with Renyi DP (RDP) composition, using delta = 1e-5 and epsilon = 10 (held fixed to match the baselines for fair comparison, not swept as a defense budget in this paper). The paper does not itself mount or evaluate any inversion/membership-inference attack against its own generator; it only cites these attacks as the motivating risk for using DP in the first place.
limitations: The paper's own text states improvements on MNIST are 'not obvious' because MNIST is already near a quality ceiling for existing methods, and that 'the quality of the generated samples appears to have reached the bottleneck' on the evaluated real datasets. CelebA experiments used only 32x32 resolution and a single binary label (gender), a simplification carried over from the DP-GAN-DPAC baseline rather than a new contribution. The authors state their noise-injection approach does not reduce blurriness much, partly attributing this to the small 32x32 image size and to noise injection itself potentially limiting the model's ability to generate sharper images. Epsilon and delta were fixed to match baselines rather than explored across a range, so no privacy-budget/utility trade-off curve is reported. No attack evaluation (e.g., empirical membership-inference or inversion success rate against the released generator) was performed to substantiate the DP guarantee's practical protection beyond the theoretical (epsilon, delta) accounting.

datasets_used: CelebA (202,599 images, 40 attributes; binary 'gender' label used; resized to 32x32x3; split 162,770 train / 19,867 val / 19,962 test)
quantitative_results: Table 1 IS/FID, epsilon=10, delta=1e-5: Ours MNIST IS 9.74 FID 49.41; Ours F-MNIST IS 6.72 FID 83.48; Ours CelebA IS 2.26 FID 114.03
Table 1 baseline DP-GAN-DPAC: MNIST IS 9.71 FID 54.06; F-MNIST IS 6.60 FID 90.77; CelebA IS 1.90 FID 139.99
Table 1 GS-WGAN: MNIST IS 9.23 FID 61.34; F-MNIST IS 5.32 FID 131.34; CelebA IS 1.85 FID 187.36
Table 1 DPSinkhorn: MNIST FID 55.56; F-MNIST FID 129.40; CelebA FID 168.40 (IS not reported)
Table 1 Real data reference: MNIST IS 9.80 FID 1.02; F-MNIST IS 8.98 FID 1.49; CelebA IS 2.77 FID 1.06
Table 2 gen2real accuracy (MLP/CNN): Ours MNIST 0.86/0.85, F-MNIST 0.74/0.70, CelebA 0.80/0.88

Table 3 noise-injection ablation (IS/FID): baseline sigma_noise=0.0 MNIST 9.71/54.6, F-MNIST 6.60/90.77, CelebA 1.90/139.99; sigma_noise=0.1 MNIST 9.60/49.41, F-MNIST 6.77/85.58, CelebA 2.37/97.70; sigma_noise=1.0 MNIST 9.27/46.84, F-MNIST 6.36/102.62, CelebA 2.98/218.38
Table 4 reconstruction-loss ablation (IS/FID): baseline gamma_recon=0.0 same as Table 3 baseline; gamma_recon=1.0 MNIST 9.74/51.63, F-MNIST 6.63/94.99, CelebA 2.06/99.10
Fixed privacy budget used throughout: epsilon=10, delta=1e-5; noise injection coefficient sigma_noise=0.1 and reconstruction coefficient gamma_recon=1.0 selected via grid search for the main results
baselines_compared: DP-GAN-DPAC (Chen et al., CVPR 2023) -- the paper's primary state-of-the-art baseline
Real data (upper-bound reference, not a private method)
